what_it_does: This file is a pre-implementation research and design note that maps how to wire OpenWebUI as a human front-end onto the Company fabric. It disambiguates two channel subsystems, answers whether the fabric can deliver inbound messages to OpenWebUI, surveys reachable HTTP/MCP/supervisor surfaces, and proposes phased integration paths.
observations: The file explicitly distinguishes 'CC-Channels' (cross-session live-injection transport) from 'Session-Channels' (named-channel/agent-session mail intents).
It concludes that the supervisor can only deliver to sessions it owns and has no route to register an arbitrary external mailbox or callback URL.
The bridge HTTP API on port 8770 exposes /api/channels, /api/sessions, /api/channel-history, /api/channel/post, /api/inbox, /api/board, /api/transcript-search, /api/chat, etc.
The bridge currently has no CORS headers and only a process-scoped operator-token gate on a few consequential routes, so browser-side calls are blocked without server-side proxying.

OpenWebUI can receive one-way fire-and-forget message injection via POST /api/v1/chats/{chatId}/messages/{messageId}/event using an admin/service-account API key, but interactive __event_call__ is not available to external callers.

It proposes four feasibility paths: P0 poll + bridge Tools (no fabric change), P1 mcpo tool surface, P2 webhook transport with a labelled fabric member (recommended), and P3 Supabase Realtime (currently stubbed).
All claims are tagged as Observed, Inferred, or Verified, with source references like file:line.
standouts: The crux answer: inbound delivery to OpenWebUI is feasible without making it a Claude Code session, but the fabric will not push for free; the cheapest path is polling /api/channel-history and the cleanest first-class path is a new 'webhook' transport plus a labelled member registration.
The two 'channel' concepts have the same word but completely different semantics, stores, transports, and MCP tools.
OpenWebUI's chat-event injection endpoint exists and is one-way, matching the 'show me the reply' use case.
The bridge lacks CORS, forcing server-side OpenWebUI Tools/Pipes rather than browser fetch.
conventions: Uses evidence tags: Observed (in code), Inferred (pattern), Verified (none here).
Cites sources as file:line and web links.
Structures analysis with tables, numbered sections, and a final path-comparison matrix.
Follows a research-note format: lead question, status, findings, recommendation, sources.
concerns: No end-to-end verification was performed; all claims are from reading code and docs.
Line-number references to a moving codebase may become stale.
The Supabase Realtime subscriber transport is described as stubbed, making the P3 path speculative.
The bridge's missing CORS and coarse operator-token gate are treated as current limitations that may need addressing for richer browser integration.
notes: A research brief that tells a lead exactly how OpenWebUI can talk to the fabric today (outbound + polling) and what small additive change is needed for true push inbound (a webhook transport and labelled member registration).
questions: What is the exact OpenWebUI admin API key scope and the precise JSON payload shape for chat-event injection?
Can the company MCP servers actually launch and operate under mcpo without modification?
How would a webhook transport registration be persisted, authorized, and rotated securely?
Which specific channels should register 'openwebui' as a member, and how would membership be managed?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose—research integration surfaces and settle whether inbound delivery to OpenWebUI is possible—matches the content exactly.
apparent_themes: Decouple front-ends from the fabric via durable mail/event logs rather than live process injection.
Extend the existing two-transport router additively rather than rewriting it.
Use server-side OpenWebUI Tools/Pipes to bridge HTTP APIs that lack browser CORS.
Distinguish live-injection semantics from named-channel mail semantics.
intention_signals: It is trying to design a non-invasive integration where OpenWebUI becomes a symmetric fabric participant—sending and receiving on the same channel thread—without requiring OpenWebUI to be a Claude Code session or the supervisor to support arbitrary external mailboxes.
novelty: It is distinct in mapping the two channel systems side-by-side and proposing a minimal third 'webhook' transport as the canonical first-class participant path, while also combining mcpo, bridge polling, and OpenWebUI event injection into one phased roadmap.
contribution: It contributes a decision framework and phased roadmap for OpenWebUI↔fabric connectivity, identifies the correct channel subsystem for a front-end integration, and specifies the small additive fabric change needed for push inbound delivery.
summary_for_embedding: A pre-implementation research note analyzing OpenWebUI integration with the Company fabric, distinguishing two channel systems, evaluating inbound and outbound integration surfaces, and proposing a phased path from polling to a new webhook transport.
intention_for_embedding: To determine whether and how OpenWebUI can act as a first-class human participant in Company fabric channels.
